questions.prompt: You’re in a working session where a real decision needs to be made. You’re sitting with a small group—some experienced, some not—and everyone has input, but no one is actually taking ownership. The conversation is starting to circle, and time is slipping. You can feel that if nothing changes, this meeting will end with no real outcome. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Step back and assess everything before committing
Push toward a decision so things keep moving
Ask questions to understand how everyone is thinking
Create structure so the decision becomes clearer
Adjust based on what seems most practical

questions.prompt: You’re working with a client who seemed ready to move forward, but now they’ve slowed down. They’re responding less, asking vague questions, and seem unsure. You know if this drifts too long, the opportunity could disappear. You can feel hesitation, but you’re not entirely sure what’s causing it. What do you do next?

options.text: Reframe the situation so the decision feels clearer
Walk through details again to reduce uncertainty
Give them space and let them come back
Push forward and try to move things along
Ask deeper questions to uncover what’s really going on

questions.prompt: It’s the middle of a hectic evening at home. One thing turns into three—something spills, someone needs attention, and another problem shows up right behind it. It’s not a crisis, but it’s enough to feel chaotic. Everyone is reacting slightly differently, and there’s no clear order. What’s your natural response in that moment?

options.text: Focus on the people first and make sure everyone is okay
Pause and take in what’s happening before reacting
Jump in and take control of the situation immediately
Try to organize things so everything settles down
Handle what’s most urgent and let the rest follow

questions.prompt: You arrive at a hotel with your family after a long travel day. The front desk agent seems overwhelmed, there’s a language barrier, and you’re told your room was downgraded. Your kids are asking about the pool, your partner looks frustrated, and the situation feels slightly off. What do you do?

options.text: Adapt quickly and make the best of the situation
Push to get the original room or a better resolution
Work through the details calmly to find a solution
Accept it and shift focus to enjoying the trip
Focus on keeping everyone else calm and settled

questions.prompt: You’re facing a decision that matters, but there’s no immediate deadline. You could act now, or you could take more time to think it through. There are trade-offs either way, and no one is pressuring you. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Talk it through with someone to get perspective
Make a decision and move forward quickly
Let it sit until clarity comes naturally
Gather more information before deciding

questions.prompt: You’re sitting in a meeting that’s dragging. The discussion keeps looping, people are repeating themselves, and nothing is moving forward. You can feel the energy dropping in the room. What do you do?

options.text: Try to align people and bring clarity to the group
Step in and push toward a decision
Reframe the conversation in a new direction
Let the discussion play out

questions.prompt: You’re in a conversation where someone challenges your idea in front of others. The tone isn’t aggressive, but it’s direct enough to shift the room’s attention. You can feel the moment tighten slightly. What do you do?

options.text: Evaluate their point and adjust if needed
Defend your position clearly
Address it later in a different setting
Ask questions to better understand their perspective

questions.prompt: You think back to a missed opportunity—something that could have gone differently if you had taken a different approach. It’s not painful, but it sticks with you. What do you naturally do with that memory?

options.text: Break it down to understand what happened
Adjust your approach moving forward
Move on and focus ahead
Talk it through with someone

questions.prompt: Your day starts falling apart—things are delayed, priorities shift, and nothing is going exactly how you planned. It’s not catastrophic, but it’s enough to disrupt momentum. What do you do?

options.text: Reprioritize and take control of what matters most
Adapt quickly and keep moving
Push through and get as much done as possible
Restructure your plan to regain control

questions.prompt: You’re attending an event where you don’t know many people. The environment is new, the conversations are already happening, and you’re deciding how to engage. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Start conversations and engage directly
Observe first and understand the room
Look for patterns in how people interact
Connect with a few people one-on-one

questions.prompt: You’re at a weekend family gathering where multiple conversations are happening at once. One person is telling a story that’s going nowhere, another is trying to make a point, and a few people are quietly disengaging. You can feel the energy drifting. No one has taken control of the moment, but it’s starting to matter. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Let it play out and see where it goes
Step in and shift the conversation direction
Listen closely and read the room before acting
Engage a few people individually to shift energy
Ask a question that refocuses the group

questions.prompt: You’re working on a project where expectations weren’t clearly defined. Now deadlines are approaching and different people have different interpretations of what success looks like. Tension is starting to build subtly. There’s still time to fix it, but only if someone takes action. What do you do?

options.text: Clarify expectations and create structure quickly
Adapt to what seems to be working and move forward
Drive toward a result and align people along the way
Talk through differences to understand perspectives
Step back and reassess before committing to direction

questions.prompt: You’re planning a trip with friends or family. Everyone has opinions—where to go, what to do, how much to spend—and none of them fully align. The planning process is becoming slower than expected, and decisions aren’t getting made. What’s your natural role in that situation?

options.text: Help people align and find common ground
Take charge and move decisions forward
Go with the flow and adjust as things evolve
Step back and evaluate the best overall option
Organize options so the group can decide clearly

questions.prompt: You’re halfway through a long day and something unexpected disrupts your schedule completely. What you planned is no longer realistic, and you need to adjust quickly. You have limited time to think, but you still need to move forward. What do you do?

options.text: Adapt quickly and move with the new reality
Rebuild a plan that makes sense under pressure
Take control and push through the disruption
Pause briefly to assess before acting
Check in with others affected before deciding

questions.prompt: You’re in a conversation where someone is explaining something, but it’s not clear, and it’s taking longer than it should. Others seem confused, but no one is saying it. You can feel the conversation losing clarity. What do you do?

options.text: Ask questions to clarify what they mean
Wait to see if clarity comes on its own
Interrupt and redirect the conversation
Break down the issue into something clearer
Let it continue and adjust internally

questions.prompt: You’re watching someone make a decision you believe is flawed. It doesn’t directly impact you, but it will affect the outcome of a shared situation. You’re deciding whether to step in or let it play out. What do you do?

options.text: Step in and address it directly
Let it play out and adapt later
Ask questions to help them reconsider
Observe and gather more information first
Suggest a more structured approach

questions.prompt: You’re learning something new in an environment where others seem to pick it up faster than you. There’s no pressure externally, but internally you’re aware of the gap. How do you respond?

options.text: Slow down and focus on understanding deeply
Ask others how they’re approaching it
Push yourself to catch up quickly
Create a system to learn it efficiently
Adapt and trust the process over time

questions.prompt: You’re driving somewhere familiar, but traffic forces you off your usual route. Now you have to decide quickly how to adjust without overthinking it. There’s no perfect option, just better or worse ones. What do you do?

options.text: Adjust on the fly and keep moving
Choose a route that feels structured and reliable
Pause briefly and consider alternatives
Pick a direction quickly and commit
Check input from others or tools before deciding

questions.prompt: You’re in a group where one person is dominating the conversation. Others are disengaging, but no one is stepping in. The dynamic is becoming unbalanced. What do you do?

options.text: Redirect attention to include others
Observe and wait for the moment to shift
Interrupt and take control of the conversation
Adapt and engage where possible
Shift structure so more voices are included

questions.prompt: You have an open block of time with no obligations. You could rest, plan, work, or do something spontaneous. Nothing is pulling you in a specific direction. What do you naturally gravitate toward?

options.text: Use the time productively and move something forward
Let the time unfold without forcing structure
Organize or plan something useful
Connect with someone or do something relational
Follow whatever feels right in the moment

questions.prompt: You’re at a dinner with people who all know each other better than you. The conversation has inside jokes, subtle dynamics, and a rhythm you’re not fully part of yet. You can either insert yourself quickly or take your time figuring things out. You feel that moment where you decide how you’re going to show up. What do you do?

options.text: Listen first and understand the dynamic
Connect with one person instead of the whole group
Jump in and establish your presence early
Observe patterns before deciding how to engage
Ask a question that shifts the conversation

questions.prompt: You’re given responsibility for something important, but the instructions are vague and expectations are unclear. No one is hovering over you, but the outcome matters. You can either define it yourself or wait for more clarity. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Create your own structure and define success
Move forward and figure it out as you go
Pause and try to understand the bigger picture
Ask for clarity before moving
Adjust as things unfold

questions.prompt: You’re in a situation where someone is upset, but they’re not saying it directly. Their tone is slightly off, and their behavior is different, but nothing explicit has been said. You can either address it or let it pass. What do you do?

options.text: Notice it but wait to see if it surfaces
Ask directly if something is wrong
Adjust your behavior and move forward
Address it directly and move through it
Read the situation before deciding

questions.prompt: You’re balancing multiple priorities, and all of them feel important. There’s no perfect solution—whatever you choose means something else gets less attention. The pressure isn’t overwhelming, but it’s real. What do you do?

options.text: Choose and commit quickly
Organize priorities logically
Think through trade-offs carefully
Adjust dynamically as things evolve
Consider how it affects others before deciding

questions.prompt: You’re working with someone whose style is completely different from yours. Their pace, communication, and approach don’t match how you operate. It’s not wrong—just different—and it’s affecting how things get done. What do you do?

options.text: Try to understand and adapt to them
Adjust where needed but keep your style
Maintain your approach and expect alignment
Create a structure that works for both
Observe and adjust over time

questions.prompt: You’re faced with an opportunity that could be valuable, but it’s unclear and comes with uncertainty. There’s potential upside, but also risk. You don’t have all the information you’d like. What do you do?

options.text: Move forward and take the opportunity
Evaluate carefully before deciding
Gather more information first
Adjust based on how it develops
Talk it through with someone before acting

questions.prompt: You’re in a conversation where the other person keeps going off track. The discussion is drifting, and the original purpose is getting lost. You can either redirect or let it continue. What do you do?

options.text: Redirect the conversation clearly
Structure the conversation back on track
Adapt and follow where it goes
Ask questions to guide it back
Let it play out for now

questions.prompt: You’re reflecting on a situation that didn’t go the way you expected. It wasn’t a failure, but it didn’t land how you wanted. You have the chance to learn from it or move on. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Think through it before taking action
Talk it through with someone
Move forward quickly
Analyze it to understand what happened
Adjust your approach next time

questions.prompt: You’re part of a group decision where no option is clearly right. Everyone has input, but there’s no strong direction. You can either push, guide, or step back. What do you do?

options.text: Create a clearer structure for decision-making
Push toward a decision
Wait and see how things unfold
Help people align on direction
Adapt to the direction that emerges

questions.prompt: You have unexpected free time and no obligations. You can choose to rest, work, connect, or explore something new. Nothing is pulling you in a specific direction. What do you do?

options.text: Follow whatever feels right
Organize or plan something useful
Use the time to move something forward
Let your mind wander without structure
Reach out or connect with someone

questions.prompt: It’s a Saturday morning at home. No strict plans, but a handful of things could get done—errands, projects, family time, or just relaxing. Everyone seems to be in a slightly different mood, and there’s no clear direction for the day yet. You can feel that whatever happens next will shape the tone of the day. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Check in with others and align on a plan
Let the day unfold naturally
Take the lead and set direction
Organize priorities into a loose plan
Adjust based on what feels right in the moment

questions.prompt: You’re having a conversation with someone close to you, and something they say doesn’t sit right. It’s not a big issue, but it creates a subtle tension. You can either address it or move past it. The moment is small, but it could matter later. What do you do?

options.text: Let it go and move forward
Ask about it to understand their perspective
Address it directly in the moment
Adjust internally and observe
Think about it before deciding how to respond

questions.prompt: You’re helping plan something important—an event, a project, or a gathering—and there are a lot of moving parts. Some people are contributing, others are waiting for direction, and details are starting to slip through the cracks. There’s still time to fix it, but it requires someone to step in. What do you do?

options.text: Create structure to bring everything together
Step in and take control of the situation
Work with others to align efforts
Pause and assess before acting
Adjust and handle things as they come

questions.prompt: You’re in a situation where plans change suddenly—something gets canceled, delayed, or shifted—and now you have to adjust quickly. It’s not ideal, but it’s not catastrophic either. You can either resist it or adapt. What do you do?

options.text: Adapt quickly and move forward
Rebuild a plan that makes sense
Take a moment to process before acting
Push forward and take control
Check in with others before deciding

questions.prompt: You’re talking with someone who has a completely different opinion than you on something that matters. The conversation is respectful, but there’s a clear disagreement. You can either push your view, explore theirs, or step back. What do you do?

options.text: Ask questions to understand their perspective
Stand firm and explain your position
Listen and consider before responding
Adapt and find common ground
Break down the issue logically

questions.prompt: You’re faced with a decision where the outcome affects more than just you—family, team, or others around you. There’s no perfect answer, just different trade-offs. You feel the weight of the decision. What do you do?

options.text: Make the decision and move forward
Evaluate details carefully before deciding
Consider how it impacts others first
Take time to think it through
Adjust based on how things evolve

questions.prompt: You’re working on something where progress is slower than expected. It’s not failing, but it’s not moving at the pace you’d like. You can either push harder, adjust your approach, or step back. What do you do?

options.text: Push forward and increase momentum
Pause and reassess your approach
Create a better structure for progress
Adjust and keep moving
Talk it through with someone

questions.prompt: You’re spending time with people in a relaxed setting—maybe a dinner, a small gathering, or time at home. There’s no pressure, but the way you engage still shapes the experience. What do you naturally do?

options.text: Focus on connection and interaction
Observe and engage naturally
Go with the flow of the moment
Drive the energy and direction
Keep things structured and smooth

questions.prompt: You’re reflecting on your own behavior in a recent situation—something where you could have handled things differently. You’re deciding what to do with that realization. What do you do?

options.text: Think about it before acting
Analyze what happened in detail
Move forward and adjust quickly
Talk it through with someone
Let it inform how you adapt going forward

questions.prompt: You have a moment alone—no distractions, no immediate tasks, nothing demanding your attention. Your mind starts to move. What naturally happens?

options.text: Start thinking about what needs to get done
Let your thoughts wander freely
Organize or plan something mentally
Think about people or conversations
Shift between thoughts without forcing direction
